category.label: Functions and Graphs
category.description: Core function concept and notation, evaluating functions from rules/graphs/tables, domain and range, piecewise-defined functions, average rate of change, basic graph symmetry, and interval notation. Fills the empty 'functions' and 'piecewise_functions' learn_category gap and supplies the prerequisite function-literacy layer for all AP Precalculus and Calc AB work.
category.yield_rationale: Function notation and evaluation underlie every AP Precalc topic (1.1–1.14, 2.3–2.14, 3.1–3.10) and every Calc AB unit; domain/range and piecewise functions recur heavily in FRQ 1 (function concepts) and limit/continuity work; average rate of change seeds AP Precalc 1.1–1.4 and Calc AB Unit 2.

umbrellas.label: The Function Concept
umbrellas.description: What makes a relation a function: the vertical-line test, input-output mapping, and the distinction between relations that are and are not functions across representations (mappings, tables, graphs, equations).
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Function vs. non-function identification appears in AP Precalc MC and grounds all function-analysis tasks in Units 1–3 and Calc AB; FRQ 1 (function concepts) assumes this fluency.

in_depth.label: Relation vs. function definition
in_depth.description: A function assigns exactly one output to each input; tests identifying whether a given rule, set of ordered pairs, or mapping diagram satisfies the uniqueness-of-output criterion.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Definitional anchor — tested directly in introductory MC items and prerequisite for all downstream function work.

in_depth.label: Vertical-line test on a graph
in_depth.description: Applying the vertical-line test to a coordinate-plane graph to determine whether the graph represents a function; tests the visual equivalent of the uniqueness-of-output criterion.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical identification of functions appears in MC; slightly lower than definition item because it is a single graphical move.

in_depth.label: Identifying a function from a table
in_depth.description: Determining whether an input-output table represents a function by checking for repeated x-values paired with different y-values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Table representation of functions recurs throughout AP Precalc FRQ 2 data contexts and rate-of-change questions; rescored to reflect genuinely lower utility relative to high-yield anchors.

in_depth.label: Deciding if an equation defines a function
in_depth.description: Using algebraic reasoning (e.g., solving for y) to determine whether an equation defines y as a function of x; distinguishes cases like y = x² (yes) from x = y² (no).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Algebraic function-identification; less frequent than graphical/tabular checks but essential for implicit-form awareness; rescored to reflect low AP exam frequency.

in_depth.label: One-to-one functions and the horizontal-line test
in_depth.description: A function is one-to-one if every output is produced by at most one input; tests applying the horizontal-line test to a graph and recognising which function families are inherently one-to-one.
in_depth.yield_rationale: One-to-one is the prerequisite gate for invertibility (P2 2.8) and is referenced in FRQ 1; higher yield than basic function identification because it feeds a heavily tested AP concept.

umbrellas.label: Function Notation and Language
umbrellas.description: Reading and using f(x) notation correctly: interpreting f(a) as the output of f at input a, naming functions with multiple letters, and translating between verbal descriptions and symbolic function notation.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: f(x) notation is the symbolic language of every AP Precalc and Calc AB topic; every FRQ and the vast majority of MC items assume fluent notation reading; extremely high downstream load.

in_depth.label: Reading f(x) notation
in_depth.description: Interpreting the expression f(x) as 'the output of function f at input x'; distinguishing f, x, and f(x) as three distinct objects, and reading statements like f(3) = 7 correctly.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Notation fluency is the entry fee for every AP math question; extremely high foundational load.

in_depth.label: Evaluating f(a) at a specific numeric input
in_depth.description: Substituting a given number for the input variable in a function rule and simplifying to find the output value; covers integer, fraction, and negative inputs.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Highest-frequency foundational move — used in function evaluation, composition setup, and rate-of-change calculation across all units and both courses.

in_depth.label: Evaluating f(expression) — input as an algebraic expression
in_depth.description: Substituting an algebraic expression (e.g., f(x+h) or f(2x)) into a function rule and simplifying; tests correct distribution and term tracking when the input itself contains variables.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Direct prerequisite for difference-quotient work in Calc AB Unit 2 and composition setup in AP Precalc 2.7; very high downstream demand.

in_depth.label: Reading function output values from a graph
in_depth.description: Given the graph of f, identifying f(a) as the y-coordinate of the point where x = a; tests reading specific output values from a coordinate-plane curve.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graph-reading of function values is required in AP Precalc FRQ 1 and throughout Calc AB limit/derivative graphical questions.

in_depth.label: Reading function output values from a table
in_depth.description: Finding f(a) by locating the input a in the input column of a table and reading the corresponding output; distinguishing this from interpolating values not in the table.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Tabular function reading recurs in AP Precalc FRQ 2 data tasks and Calc AB rate-of-change and accumulation table problems.

in_depth.label: Solving f(x) = c — finding inputs from outputs
in_depth.description: Given a function value (output), finding all inputs x such that f(x) = c by reading a graph, scanning a table, or solving an equation; tests the reversal direction of function evaluation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Solving f(x) = c is the reverse of evaluation and directly underlies inverse function concepts (P2 2.8) and zero-finding (P1 1.8); high cross-unit demand.

umbrellas.label: Domain and Range
umbrellas.description: Identifying the set of valid inputs (domain) and possible outputs (range) of a function from its rule, graph, or table; applying algebraic restrictions such as division by zero and square roots of negatives.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Domain and range are explicitly tested in AP Precalc 2.3, 2.11, 3.4, FRQ 1, and throughout Calc AB continuity and integration limit analysis; a top-three foundational concept by frequency.

in_depth.label: Reading domain from a graph
in_depth.description: Identifying all x-values for which a graph has defined output by scanning the graph horizontally; recognising open/closed endpoints and gaps from graphical features.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical domain reading is required in AP Precalc FRQ 1 and MC graph-analysis items; also foundational for Calc AB continuity and limit analysis.

in_depth.label: Reading range from a graph
in_depth.description: Identifying all y-values attained by a function by scanning the graph vertically; distinguishing bounded ranges (e.g., sinusoidal) from unbounded ones.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Range identification is consistently tested in AP Precalc MC and FRQ 1; slightly less frequent than domain due to the asymmetry in how functions are defined.

in_depth.label: Domain restrictions from algebraic rules
in_depth.description: Finding the natural domain of a function rule by excluding inputs that cause division by zero (denominator = 0) or even-root of a negative (radicand < 0); covers square-root and rational function cases.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Algebraic domain restrictions appear in every unit involving rational or radical functions (P1 1.8–1.10, P2 2.3) and are tested in both MC and FRQ contexts; one of the highest-frequency domain skills.

in_depth.label: Determining range from a function rule
in_depth.description: Using algebraic reasoning (completing the square, vertex analysis, asymptote identification) to determine the range of a function from its symbolic form, without a graph.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Algebraic range determination is moderately tested in AP Precalc MC; lower frequency than domain because it often requires deeper analysis.

in_depth.label: Domain restricted by real-world context
in_depth.description: Restricting the mathematical domain to values that make physical or contextual sense (e.g., only non-negative time, positive lengths); tests reading implicit constraints from a word problem.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Context-restricted domain appears in AP Precalc 1.13 (model assumption articulation) and FRQ 2 modeling tasks; also in Calc AB applied optimization.

in_depth.label: Domain and range from a table of values
in_depth.description: Listing the domain as the set of input values and the range as the set of output values in a finite input-output table; recognising that repeated outputs do not affect range cardinality.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Table-based domain/range is lower-stakes than graphical or algebraic but tests careful reading; used in AP Precalc data-table MC items; rescored to reflect genuinely low exam frequency.

umbrellas.label: Interval Notation
umbrellas.description: Writing and interpreting domain, range, and solution sets using interval notation with parentheses, brackets, and infinity symbols; converting between inequality notation, set-builder notation, and interval notation.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Interval notation is the universal language for domain/range answers throughout AP Precalc and Calc AB; F3 groundwork (inequality notation) feeds directly into this; every domain/range item implicitly requires it.

in_depth.label: Open vs. closed interval endpoints
in_depth.description: Distinguishing parentheses (exclusive endpoint) from brackets (inclusive endpoint) in interval notation; connecting to strict vs. non-strict inequalities and open vs. closed dots on graphs.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Endpoint notation precision is tested every time domain/range or continuity intervals appear; fundamental to correct communication in AP math.

in_depth.label: Converting inequalities to interval notation
in_depth.description: Rewriting a linear inequality or compound inequality as an interval (e.g., 2 ≤ x < 5 → [2, 5)) or as a union of intervals; covers all four basic inequality forms and their interval equivalents.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Direct conversion skill used whenever domain/range or solution sets must be expressed; AP Precalc FRQ scoring expects correct interval notation.

in_depth.label: Union and intersection in interval notation
in_depth.description: Writing domains or solution sets that consist of multiple disjoint intervals using the union symbol (∪); interpreting a union as 'either interval'; distinguishes from intersections.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Union notation arises whenever a function has a gap in its domain (e.g., rational function with one excluded value) and is required for AP Precalc domain MC answers.

in_depth.label: Intervals extending to infinity
in_depth.description: Writing unbounded domains or ranges using (−∞, a) or (b, ∞) notation, always with parentheses at ±∞; testing awareness that infinity is not a number and never gets a bracket.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Unbounded intervals appear in polynomial/rational domain expressions (P1) and throughout Calc AB; infinity-endpoint convention is a common precision error on AP exams.

in_depth.label: Writing interval notation from a graph
in_depth.description: Reading the domain or range of a graphed function and expressing it in interval notation; requires combining graph-reading skill with correct bracket/parenthesis convention for open/closed endpoints.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Composite graphical + notation skill; appears in AP Precalc FRQ 1 (function concepts) where students must state domain/range from a given graph.

umbrellas.label: Reading and Interpreting Graphs
umbrellas.description: Extracting and interpreting mathematical information from coordinate-plane graphs: increasing/decreasing intervals, local maxima and minima, intercepts, end behavior reading, and describing graph features in precise language.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Graph reading underlies AP Precalc topics 1.1–1.4 (covariation, rates of change from graphs), 2.3, 2.11, 3.4, and virtually every FRQ; also the primary mode of Calc AB limit and derivative questions.

in_depth.label: Identifying increasing and decreasing intervals
in_depth.description: Locating intervals where a function's graph rises (increasing) or falls (decreasing) as x increases from left to right; expressing these intervals using interval notation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Increasing/decreasing identification is tested in AP Precalc 1.1 (covariation) and 1.2 (rates of change) and recurs in Calc AB derivative sign analysis.

in_depth.label: Identifying local maxima and minima from a graph
in_depth.description: Locating the x- and y-coordinates of local maximum and minimum points by finding peaks and valleys on a graph; distinguishing local from global (absolute) extrema.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Local extrema identification recurs in AP Precalc 1.2–1.4 and is a pillar of Calc AB Unit 5 (analytical applications); graphical identification is the foundation before algebraic methods.

in_depth.label: Reading x-intercepts and y-intercepts from a graph
in_depth.description: Identifying where a graph crosses the x-axis (zeros of f) and the y-axis (value of f at x = 0) by reading the coordinate-plane graph; naming intercepts as ordered pairs or single values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Intercept reading is among the most tested graphical skills across AP Precalc (1.6–1.10, 2.11, 3.4) and Calc AB; direct prerequisite for zero-finding and y-intercept interpretation.

in_depth.label: Describing end behavior from a graph
in_depth.description: Reading and describing what a function's graph approaches as x → ∞ and x → −∞ using limit-style language or arrow notation; distinguishes functions that grow without bound from those that approach horizontal asymptotes.
in_depth.yield_rationale: End-behavior description from a graph is prerequisite for AP Precalc 1.6–1.7 (polynomial/rational end behavior) and Calc AB limit analysis at infinity.

in_depth.label: Interpreting a function's values over an interval from a graph
in_depth.description: Describing how output values behave over a specified x-interval: whether the function is positive, negative, increasing, decreasing, or constant, and by approximately how much it changes.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Interval-level graph interpretation supports AP Precalc covariation (1.1) and rate-of-change (1.2) questions and Calc AB mean-value/accumulation reasoning.

in_depth.label: Precise coordinate reading from a graph
in_depth.description: Reading the exact or approximate coordinates of labeled points on a graph, including non-integer values; tests attention to axis scale and whether specific coordinates are explicitly marked vs. estimated.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Precision in coordinate reading matters for rate-of-change calculations from graphs and for correctly stating function values; moderate direct yield but affects accuracy across many questions.

umbrellas.label: Tables and Numerical Input-Output Relationships
umbrellas.description: Working with functions presented as input-output tables: reading values, identifying patterns, computing differences and ratios between consecutive outputs, and recognising the representation's limitations compared to continuous graphs.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Table representation of functions is tested in AP Precalc FRQ 2 (modeling from data) and MC tabular questions; Calc AB uses tables heavily in rate-of-change, accumulation, and FRQ data contexts.

in_depth.label: Reading input-output pairs from a table
in_depth.description: Locating a specific input in a table and reading its paired output; also finding all inputs that produce a given output; tests basic table navigation for a function presented in tabular form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Baseline table-reading skill; required before any rate-of-change or difference calculation from a table.

in_depth.label: Computing consecutive changes (differences) from a table
in_depth.description: Calculating the difference between successive output values for equally or unequally spaced inputs; recognising whether outputs change by a constant amount (linear pattern) or a constant ratio (exponential pattern).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Consecutive differences underlie AP Precalc 1.3 (linear vs. quadratic rates) and 2.1 (arithmetic vs. geometric); a direct prerequisite for average rate of change from tables.

in_depth.label: Computing consecutive output differences (Δy) and classifying linearity
in_depth.description: For a table with equally spaced inputs, computing the first differences (Δy = y_{i+1} − y_i) between successive output values and identifying whether they are constant (implying a linear relationship) or non-constant (implying non-linearity); a pure table-reading skill that does not require computing a rate per unit of input.
in_depth.yield_rationale: First-difference pattern recognition is a distinct table-reading skill from AROC computation: it reads the raw output change without dividing by Δx; directly tested in AP Precalc 1.3 linearity identification and FRQ 2 model-selection items.

in_depth.label: Domain and range from a table
in_depth.description: Listing the domain as all input values in the table and the range as all distinct output values; recognising that a finite table represents only a restricted sample of the full function.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Low-complexity but frequently tested as part of domain/range questions when a table is given; lower yield because it reduces to simple list-making; rescored to reflect genuinely low utility relative to high-yield anchors.

in_depth.label: Connecting table values to a corresponding graph
in_depth.description: Plotting or identifying graphical features consistent with given table values — for example, locating which graph passes through all the tabulated points or determining where the function is increasing based on the table.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Cross-representation translation between table and graph is a Mathematical Practice (MP2) skill tested in AP Precalc MC and is prerequisite for model validation (2.6).

umbrellas.label: Piecewise-Defined Functions
umbrellas.description: Writing, reading, evaluating, and graphing piecewise-defined functions; determining which piece applies for a given input; constructing piecewise definitions from graphs.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Piecewise functions are a named gap in the DB (empty 'piecewise_functions' category) and appear in Calc AB Unit 1 limit/continuity questions and AP Precalc 2.6 (model validation); also tested in AP Precalc MC involving domain-restricted behavior.

in_depth.label: Evaluating a piecewise function at a specific input
in_depth.description: Selecting the correct branch of a piecewise definition for a given input value by checking the domain condition for each piece, then evaluating that branch's rule at the input.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Direct evaluation is the most-tested piecewise skill; appears in Calc AB limit problems and AP Precalc function-concept MC items.

in_depth.label: Handling the boundary value in a piecewise function
in_depth.description: Correctly applying strict vs. non-strict inequality conditions at breakpoints (e.g., which piece owns x = 2 when one piece covers x < 2 and the other x ≥ 2); tests reading inequality direction and inclusion. Note: checking whether both pieces yield the same output at a breakpoint (the continuity condition) is a Calc AB Unit 1 skill owned by limit_1_continuity_of_piecewise_functions_at_boundary.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Boundary precision is a common error point and is tested whenever piecewise functions appear with a mixture of strict and non-strict domain conditions.

in_depth.label: Graphing a piecewise-defined function
in_depth.description: Sketching the graph of a piecewise function by graphing each piece only over its specified domain interval; correctly representing open and closed endpoints at each breakpoint.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphing piecewise functions is tested in AP Precalc MC and is prerequisite for visually interpreting limits from piecewise graphs in Calc AB.

in_depth.label: Writing a piecewise definition from a graph
in_depth.description: Reading a piecewise graph and writing the algebraic rule and domain interval for each piece; requires identifying slopes, intercepts, or other features of each segment.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Writing piecewise definitions from graphs appears in AP Precalc and Calc AB FRQ contexts; tests both graph-reading and notation fluency.

in_depth.label: Domain and range of a piecewise function
in_depth.description: Determining the full domain and range of a piecewise function by taking the union of all piece domains and by identifying all output values across all branches; expressing results in interval notation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain/range of piecewise functions combines piecewise evaluation with interval notation; moderate direct yield but integrates two important skills.

umbrellas.label: Average Rate of Change
umbrellas.description: Conceptual foundations of average rate of change: the geometric meaning as secant-line slope, interpreting sign and magnitude, and comparing rates across intervals. The mechanical computation of (f(b) − f(a))/(b − a) from a formula, graph, or table is owned by AP Precalc P1 (poly_average_rate_of_change); this F7 umbrella covers the abstract concept layer that applies across all function families.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Average rate of change is the explicit focus of AP Precalc 1.2 and 1.3 and the conceptual entry point to derivatives in Calc AB Unit 2; among the most-tested foundational skills across both courses; FRQ 2 regularly requires rate-of-change computation and interpretation.

in_depth.label: AROC as slope of the secant line
in_depth.description: Interpreting average rate of change geometrically as the slope of the line connecting (a, f(a)) to (b, f(b)) on the graph; tests the equivalence between the algebraic formula and the graphical secant — purely as a precalc conceptual statement about any function, independent of limit or derivative machinery.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Secant-slope interpretation is the geometric bridge to derivative definition in Calc AB Unit 2; AP Precalc 1.2 explicitly connects AROC to covariation and graph behavior.

in_depth.label: Comparing AROC over different intervals
in_depth.description: Computing average rates of change over multiple intervals and comparing the values to describe whether a function is speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction; connects to concavity and increasing/decreasing behavior.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Interval comparison of AROC appears in AP Precalc 1.3–1.4 (linear vs. non-linear rate patterns) and is foundational for Calc AB MVT and increasing/decreasing analysis.

in_depth.label: Writing the secant-line slope expression in generic (a, b) notation
in_depth.description: Writing the symbolic expression for the slope of the secant line through two labeled points (a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)) as [f(b) − f(a)] / (b − a) using generic parameter names, without substituting a specific function rule or simplifying/expanding; tests correct symbolic setup of the ratio before any function-specific algebra.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Setting up the generic secant-slope expression is a distinct F7 skill that precedes the function-specific computation owned by P1; ensures students can write the ratio correctly for any function family before learning to compute it for polynomials.

in_depth.label: Identifying AROC from a verbally described function relationship
in_depth.description: Recognizing and computing the average rate of change when a function relationship is described only in words (e.g., 'a balloon rises 30 feet in 5 seconds, then 12 feet in 3 more seconds') without a formula, graph, or table — tests isolating the relevant output change and input change from a verbal context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Verbal-context AROC problems appear in AP Precalc FRQ 2 contextual sub-parts and test whether students can extract the Δy and Δx from a description before applying the formula; a distinct skill from formula or table computation.

in_depth.label: Interpreting the sign of AROC (positive, negative, or zero)
in_depth.description: Determining what a positive, negative, or zero average rate of change implies about the function's behavior over the interval: increasing output, decreasing output, or no net change respectively; does not require units or a real-world context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign interpretation of AROC is a foundational conceptual skill that bridges the algebraic formula to function behavior language (increasing/decreasing); tested in AP Precalc covariation items (1.1–1.2) and is a prerequisite for all contextual interpretation.

in_depth.label: AROC of zero and constant function behavior
in_depth.description: Recognizing that an average rate of change equal to zero over an interval implies the function's net output change is zero — which may reflect constant behavior, or equal rises and falls that cancel; distinguishes zero AROC from the function being identically constant.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Zero-AROC as a special case is a common MC trap (students confuse zero net change with a constant function) and connects to the MVT in Calc AB; moderate yield as a conceptual precision skill.

umbrellas.label: Even and Odd Function Symmetry
umbrellas.description: Determining whether a function is even (symmetric about the y-axis), odd (symmetric about the origin), or neither, using the algebraic test f(−x) = f(x) or f(−x) = −f(x) and by visual inspection of a graph.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Even/odd symmetry is directly tested in AP Precalc MC and FRQ 4 (symbolic) and simplifies trig identity work in P3; moderate-high yield given its cross-cutting utility.

in_depth.label: Algebraic even-function test: f(−x) = f(x)
in_depth.description: Substituting −x for x in a function rule, simplifying, and verifying whether the result equals the original f(x); identifies functions symmetric about the y-axis.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Algebraic symmetry test for even functions is a standard AP Precalc MC item and a prerequisite for recognizing cosine as even in trig unit P3.

in_depth.label: Algebraic odd-function test: f(−x) = −f(x)
in_depth.description: Substituting −x for x in a function rule and verifying whether the result equals the negation of f(x); identifies functions with 180° rotational symmetry about the origin.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Odd-function test parallels even-function test; particularly important for recognising sine as odd in P3.

in_depth.label: Identifying even/odd symmetry visually from a graph
in_depth.description: Recognising y-axis symmetry (even) or 180°-rotational symmetry about the origin (odd) by inspecting the shape of a graph; tests the graphical interpretation of the algebraic symmetry conditions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical symmetry identification is tested in AP Precalc MC graph-analysis items; rescored to reflect that visual symmetry inspection is simpler and less discriminating than the algebraic test, placing it below the set-builder notation anchor (0.20) only slightly.

in_depth.label: Classifying a function as neither even nor odd
in_depth.description: Demonstrating that a function is neither even nor odd by showing that f(−x) ≠ f(x) AND f(−x) ≠ −f(x) for some or all x; requires checking both conditions and providing a counterexample.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The 'neither' classification is a common wrong-direction trap in symmetry MC items but is the default outcome for most functions; low direct yield because it reduces to a simple negative check; rescored accordingly.

in_depth.label: Using even/odd symmetry to predict output values without computing
in_depth.description: Applying the even or odd property directly to a known function value to predict the output at a reflected input without evaluating the function rule — for example, if f is odd and f(3) = 7 then f(−3) = −7; if f is even and f(−5) = 2 then f(5) = 2.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Using symmetry to infer output values is a pure foundational skill distinct from integral simplification; tested in AP Precalc MC when only one function value and a symmetry property are given; replaces the deleted fg_symmetry_and_definite_integrals which required downstream definite integral knowledge.

umbrellas.label: Describing Function Behavior and Covariation
umbrellas.description: Using precise language and mathematical notation to describe how input and output values change together — whether the function is increasing, decreasing, or constant over an interval — and connecting this to sign of rate of change and concavity direction.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Covariation language is the explicit focus of AP Precalc 1.1 and 1.3–1.4 and feeds directly into rate-of-change reasoning in Calc AB Units 4–5; FRQ communication tasks (MP3) require precise covariation descriptions.

in_depth.label: Using precise language for increasing/decreasing behavior
in_depth.description: Describing a function's behavior using phrases like 'f is increasing on [a, b]' with correct interval notation and justification referencing how output changes as input increases; contrasts with merely saying 'the graph goes up'.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Precise covariation language is explicitly tested in AP Precalc 1.1 and in FRQ communication tasks (MP3); also prerequisite for Calc AB first-derivative sign analysis phrasing.

in_depth.label: Identifying constant behavior over an interval
in_depth.description: Recognising when a function's output does not change over an interval (slope = 0 or horizontal segment on graph) and expressing this as 'f is constant on [a, b]'.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Constant intervals complete the increasing/decreasing/constant trichotomy; lower yield because they are less prominent than non-constant behavior in AP questions and represent a simple graphical read; rescored to reflect position well below midrange anchors.

in_depth.label: Identifying concavity direction (concave up vs. down) from a graph
in_depth.description: Describing whether the graph curves upward (concave up) or downward (concave down) over an interval by visual inspection of the curve's shape alone — specifically whether the curve bends like a bowl (opening up) or an arch (opening down); this is a visual identification skill and does not involve reasoning about whether the rate of change is increasing or decreasing.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Concavity identification from a graph is prerequisite for AP Precalc 1.3 (quadratic rate behavior) and Calc AB Unit 5 (second derivative analysis); tightened to visual identification only to avoid overlap with poly_covariation_concavity_direction which owns the rate-of-change reasoning connection.

in_depth.label: Identifying where a function is positive, negative, or zero
in_depth.description: Locating intervals where f(x) > 0, f(x) < 0, or f(x) = 0 from a graph or table; tests reading the relationship between the graph and the x-axis and connects to zero-finding and inequality solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign analysis of a function output is used in AP Precalc rational-function inequality work (P1) and Calc AB accumulation/integral sign problems; moderately high yield.

in_depth.label: Identifying absolute maximum and minimum over a closed interval
in_depth.description: Finding the highest and lowest output values of a function on a closed bounded interval from a graph or table; distinguishing absolute (global) extrema from local extrema.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Absolute extrema on a closed interval are tested in AP Precalc and Calc AB Unit 5 (Extreme Value Theorem context); foundational before calculus-based optimization methods.
